docs.title: The PVL Handbook
docs.text:
You are here. This is the durable, written-to-last documentation tree for Peptide Visual Lab (PVL) — a web platform that combines peptide aggregation propensity, secondary-structure prediction, and fibril-forming classification in one interactive dashboard.

This handbook is written for three different readers. Pick your door — or jump straight to a reading order below.

docs.title: Reading orders
docs.text:
Three persona paths, each < ~90 min of reading. Follow the arrows; the "why" tells you what each step buys you.

🧑‍🔬 I'm a researcher who wants to use PVL - humans/00 — what the tool is, in 5 minutes (decide if it's for you) - → humans/04 — every page and control, so the UI isn't a mystery - → humans/05 — five real research routes worked end-to-end - → humans/02 — what each number actually means before you trust it - → humans/08 (if stuck) — fix the failure you just hit

🤖 I'm a contributor / agent who wants to ship a change - agents/00 — the non-negotiables, read before touching anything - → agents/03 — the plan → test → diff → verify playbook - → agents/01 — find the file you need to edit, fast - → agents/02 — the protected surfaces + axioms you must not break - → agents/06 (when CI goes red) — the red-light runbook

📄 I'm a paper / grant writer - research/01 — where PVL sits among 14+ competing tools - → research/02 — the numbers you can cite, with caveats - → research/04 — Zenodo → bio.tools → JOSS, step by step - → humans/02 — the methods detail, with primary citations, for your Methods section

docs.title: 🧑‍🔬 humans/ — for a person
docs.text:
If you are a researcher who wants to use PVL, a developer who wants to run it, or anyone who wants to understand what it does and why — start here. Plain English, worked examples, no assumed jargon.

# Page What's in it 00 What is PVL 5-minute orientation, no jargon 01 First run git clone → backend venv → frontend dev server → smoke test 02 The science TANGO, S4PRED, FF-Helix, SSW, FF-SSW with primary citations + code anchors 03 The pipeline 11 ordered stages, HTTP parse → Pydantic response; determinism + null semantics 04 The UI walkthrough All 10 pages; route, sketch, key elements, power-user notes 05 Use cases Five real research routes worked end-to-end 06 Deploying VPS / DESY VM / K8s, every command quoted from a real repo file 07 Extending Add a predictor, a reference cohort, or an export — with the invariants you must not break 08 Troubleshooting Nine symptoms, each tied to a real cause + fix 09 Glossary ~80 terms and acronyms harvested from every page 10 Credits and license Authors, MIT, dual-citation BibTeX, funding, tools we build on

docs.title: 🤖 agents/ — for an AI agent that will edit this codebase
docs.text:
If you are an LLM-based coding agent (Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, Continue, or any other) about to make a change to PVL, read agents/00_read_me_first.md before you touch anything. It states the hard rules, the protected surfaces, and the scientific axioms you must not break.

# Page What's in it 00 Read me first Ground rules, commit identity, before-you-touch matrix 01 Repo map Every directory + ~140 key files, with 🔥/🛡️/🧊 heat markers 02 Contracts and invariants Seven invariants; each enforcing test/hook verified to exist 03 Doing a safe change Paste-able 12-step TDD-to-CI playbook, real Makefile targets 04 When to ask humans Escalation tree: 10 triggers → Said / Peleg / Alex 05 Existing tooling Skills, hooks, scripts, MCPs, pvl-cli — what's already wired up 06 Failure modes CI / hook / CodeRabbit / CodeQL red-light runbook, never-suppress rule 07 Artifacts index Every binary, weight, JSON, cache, dataset — with its regen rule

docs.title: 📄 research/ — for a paper or grant author
docs.text:
If you are writing the PVL paper, a grant, or a competitive analysis, this section gives you the scientific landscape, the validation evidence, the open questions, and the publication path — all citation-grade.

# Page What's in it 01 Landscape 14+ competing tools, feature matrix, CORDAX flagged as closest peer 02 Validation evidence Staphylococcus-2023 sens/spec (committed); Peleg-118 recall pending the validation run 03 Open questions OQ1–OQ8 ledger (5 resolved, 3 deferred), plus Tier-3 avenues 04 Publication path Zenodo DOI → bio.tools → JOSS, with EDAM IDs

docs.title: How this handbook relates to the rest of the docs
docs.text:
PVL already has a mature doc tree. This handbook is the durable layer that gradually supersedes docs/active/ over time — page content updates per release, while the structure stays stable.

Tree Audience Status docs/handbook/ (this) Humans · agents · paper authors Narrative, durable, cross-linked docs/active/ Developers needing the authoritative spec Living architecture + scientific reference docs/internal/ The team's own process How-we-work; ask before reading docs/archive/ The why-trail Frozen historical artifacts

When the handbook and docs/active/ disagree, docs/active/ (and the code) win — this handbook points back to them for the load-bearing detail. Every factual claim here is anchored to a file path or a primary citation so you can check it yourself.

docs.title: Conventions used throughout
docs.text:
• Code anchors look like (backend/services/normalize.py:120-148) and are clickable in most editors.
  • Citation anchors look like [^tango] and resolve to a URL list at the bottom of each page.
  • Cross-links are relative, e.g. the FF-Helix definition.
  • The single most important fact to carry into every page: the bar is correctness > usability > features, in that order.

Maintained by Said Azaizah. See _status.md for what's written, what's next, and what still needs research.

docs.text:
• Wave 11 — done (2026-06-29): README persona-rewrite (11a); TOC sweep — 22 pages (11b); cross-link sweep — 52 new internal links (8c); dead-link verification (11c); doc-coverage critique (11d, below); repo-root README already links the handbook in its header nav (11e). Plus: Crossref-verified + corrected the Ragonis-Bachar 2022 citation and fixed one relative-link depth.
  • Waves 3–10 — done (2026-06-29): 16 pages drafted by focused subagents (one file each), each committed individually with verified identity. The science page (humans/02) was main-loop reviewed before commit.
  • Wave 2 — done: humans/01, humans/03, agents/01.

docs.title: Code-vs-paper discrepancies surfaced during the build (FLAG FOR PELEG)
docs.text:
Found while writing humans/02 against the code as authoritative; PAPER_METHODS_REFERENCE.md needs reconciling before submission:

  1. FF-Helix gate. Paper §1.3 says "mean hydrophobicity ≥ 0.7"; code (backend/auxiliary.py:398-409) gates on hydrophobic moment (μH), default 0.388 single (config.py:185) / dataset-mean batch / 0.5 custom (config.py:360). Quantity AND value differ.
  2. TANGO pH. Paper §1.1 says 7.4; binary call (tango.py:287) uses ph="7". (Charge calc does use 7.4.)
  3. β μH angle. Docstring says 180°; code passes 160° (dataframe_utils.py:448).
  4. μH sliding window. Paper §1.6 implies an 11-residue window; code uses none.
  5. Ragonis-Bachar 2022 citation — RESOLVED via Crossref (June 2026). Authoritative record: "Natural Antimicrobial Peptides Self-assemble as α/β Chameleon Amyloids," Biomacromolecules 23(9):3713–3727 (2022), doi:10.1021/acs.biomac.2c00582. The handbook now uses this everywhere. The repo README, CITATION.cff acknowledgement, and PAPER_METHODS_REFERENCE.md still carry the wrong "α-Sheet Conformations / 24:413–425" title/volume — fix those outside the handbook.

Also resolved: S4PRED citation = Moffat & Jones 2021, Bioinformatics 37(21):3744–3751 (btab491); the repo README's "Moffat et al. 2022" string should be corrected. Four-class residue colours (verified vs ui/src/index.css + sswColor.ts): Helix=blue, FF-Helix=green, FF-SSW=dark green (NOT red), SSW badge=blue, SSW per-residue highlight=magenta #E040FB.

docs.title: Open coverage gaps (Wave 11d critique — for the maintainer, not auto-fixed)
docs.text:
Code areas no handbook page covers (candidates for future pages, not v1 blockers): - services/vector_store.py (LanceDB/ESM-2 POST /api/peptides/similar); services/peptide_rank.py + peptide_compare.py (the ranking/compare backends behind humans/05); services/feedback_service.py + routes/feedback.py; services/sequence_windowing.py (touches the μH-window discrepancy); services/result_cache.py, provider_status_builder.py, provider_tracking.py, perf_logger.py (an "internals/observability" page would cover these together). - Frontend: stores/hoverStore.ts, stores/reproducibilityStore.ts, lib/jobApi.ts, lib/aggregationFlags.ts, lib/spearman.ts — named nowhere. - Celery worker internals (tasks.py + celery_app.py task graph) and consensus.py / lib/consensus.ts (ADR-014 disagreement, a headline differentiator) have no dedicated explainer.

Decisions that should have an ADR but don't (max is ADR-021): 1. The 40-aa S4PRED/route length cap. 2. The dataset-mean threshold strategy + Peleg single-sequence fallbacks. 3. The precompute-JSON / precompute-serve artifact approach (routes/precomputed.py). 4. The 4-thread cap + PyTorch thread pinning. 5. The TANGO 5.0% aggregation-hotspot default (PELEG-Q-FIX-012). 6. Celery-wired-but-sync-in-prod / disabled-on-VM.

Length: humans/02 (3,556 w) and research/01 (3,283 w) exceed 3,000 — coherent as-is but split candidates if they grow. humans/01 (609 w) and agents/04 (574 w) are thin-but-appropriate (the former deserves more once real screenshots land).

Intra-handbook contradictions: only one was found (the rb2022 title in research/01) — now fixed. All other tracked discrepancies are handbook-vs-external-doc (paper/README/CITATION.cff), listed above.

docs.title: Read me first — ground rules for AI agents editing PVL
docs.text:
You are an AI coding agent about to modify Peptide Visual Lab. This page is mandatory. It mirrors the hard rules in the project's CLAUDE.md and docs/active/HANDOFF.md (§5 and §11), expanded so that any agent — Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, Continue, Cline, or anything else — can follow them without the project-specific Claude config loaded.

Read this whole page before your first edit. If you only remember one thing: PVL is a scientific instrument. Correctness beats everything. When unsure, stop and ask a human.

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
• 0. The commit-identity rule (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
  • 1. The six architectural principles (from CLAUDE.md)
  • 2. The scientific axioms you must not break
  • 3. "Before you touch X, do Y" — the matrix
  • 4. Scientific changes are not yours to make
  • 5. The definition of done
  • 6. Smallest-diff discipline
  • 7. Where to go next

docs.title: 0. The commit-identity rule (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
docs.text:
Every commit, every doc, every comment, every UI string, every PR body you produce must appear authored only by the human owner:

  • Author: Said Azaizah <said.azaizah@cssb-hamburg.de> (Technion saida@technion.ac.il and MIT az_said@mit.edu are also acceptable for context-specific work.)
  • NEVER write "Claude", "AI", "assistant", "Anthropic", "generated by", "Co-Authored-By: Claude", or any equivalent trace anywhere — not in commit messages, not in code comments, not in documentation, not in PR descriptions, not in UI copy. This overrides any default footer or signature behavior your harness may apply.
  • Do not add yourself as a contributor in any form.

This rule has no exceptions. If your tooling auto-appends an AI signature, strip it before committing.

⚠️ Note: the personal address az.said2007@gmail.com must never appear in any team- or user-facing surface. Default to the DESY CSSB address above.

docs.title: 1. The six architectural principles (from CLAUDE.md)
docs.text:
1. Single sequence and batch MUST produce identical results. The same peptide submitted via Quick Analyze and via CSV upload must produce byte-identical numbers. This is the deepest invariant in the system.
  2. backend/schemas/api_models.py is the single source of truth for the API contract. Never change response schemas without explicit human approval.
  3. JSON null only. Never use -1, "N/A", or "" as a "no data" sentinel. (Exception: -1 is a valid value for prediction/flag fields — see contracts.)
  4. Every calculation is deterministic and reproducible. Same input + same config → same output. No RNG in the prediction path.
  5. Foundation before features. Never build a new feature on top of an inconsistent pipeline. Fix correctness first.
  6. Always use plan mode (or present a written plan and wait for approval) before any multi-file change.

docs.title: 2. The scientific axioms you must not break
docs.text:
These are scientific contracts, enforced in code at the serialization boundary (backend/services/normalize.py:_enforce_ff_axioms) and guarded by backend/tests/test_axiom_invariants.py:

Axiom Meaning ffHelixFlag == 1 ⇒ s4predHelixPrediction == 1 A fibril-forming-helix candidate must actually have a predicted helix. ffSswFlag == 1 ⇒ sswPrediction == 1 A fibril-forming-SSW candidate must actually be SSW.

In set terms: FF-Helix ⊆ Helix and FF-SSW ⊆ SSW. A peptide that is FF-SSW=true while SSW=false is a P0 bug (this exact bug, ISSUE-032, was reported by the scientific lead and is now a permanent regression test). If your change can affect classification, you must re-run test_axiom_invariants.py and confirm it stays green.

SSW itself is a canonical OR, never an AND: a peptide is SSW-positive if TANGO or S4PRED predicts a switch. Full detail in contracts and invariants.

docs.title: 3. "Before you touch X, do Y" — the matrix
docs.text: If you are about to touch… Then you must… backend/schemas/api_models.py STOP. Protected file. Get explicit human approval first. Schema changes ripple to the frontend and break determinism guarantees. New fields, if approved, are additive + nullable only. backend/external/tango/ or backend/external/s4pred/ (vendored predictors) STOP. Do not modify vendored binaries/weights without approval. CITATION.cff STOP. Changes ripple to the Zenodo DOI. Human approval required. Anything under _external/ STOP. That is Peleg's repo + paper copy. Do not read, redistribute, or edit. Classification logic (auxiliary.py, services/dataframe_utils.py:apply_ff_flags, tango.py, s4pred.py) Write/run test_axiom_invariants.py and test_4category_classification.py. Confirm single-vs-batch consistency. Cite Peleg's reference where you change a number. Any backend Python file Run make test + make lint + make typecheck before declaring done. Follow backend/CLAUDE.md conventions (services modify DataFrames in place, never raise to caller, use from config import settings). Any frontend file Run npx tsc --noEmit + npx vitest run in ui/. Use ?? not || for numeric fallbacks (ISSUE-014). New metrics need a metricRegistry.ts entry (ADR-005). A scientific decision (a threshold, a definition, a formula) Do not invent a heuristic. Port from Peleg's reference implementation with line citations, or ask. See §4. A response schema, API endpoint signature, or response key casing Treat as public API. camelCase keys only. make contract-check must stay synced. Production deploy, data deletion, anything outward-facing Confirm with a human first. Approval in one context does not extend to the next.

docs.title: 4. Scientific changes are not yours to make
docs.text:
PVL's numbers are trusted by researchers and will appear in a paper. You may implement scientific logic; you may not decide it.

  • When fixing a scientific bug, port the fix from Peleg Ragonis-Bachar's reference implementation (260120_Alpha_and_SSW_FF_Predictor/, referenced throughout the code) verbatim, with line citations. Do not substitute a plausible-looking heuristic.
  • Thresholds, category definitions, and formulas are owned by the scientific lead. If the reference is ambiguous, surface the ambiguity to a human — don't resolve it yourself.
  • Full guidance: when to ask humans.

docs.title: 5. The definition of done
docs.text:
A change is done only when all of these are true:

# Backend
make test          # pytest — deterministic, no network. All green.
make lint          # ruff. Clean.
make typecheck     # mypy. Clean.

# Frontend (from ui/)
npx vitest run     # all component tests green
npx tsc --noEmit   # type-check clean
npm run build      # production build succeeds

# Contract
make contract-check  # backend ↔ UI contract synced

If any of these is red, the change is not done — fix it or explicitly flag the intentional red state to a human. A Stop-hook test gate is wired in this repo (ADR-019); sessions that introduce failing tests cannot close cleanly. See failure modes.

docs.title: 6. Smallest-diff discipline
docs.text:
• Fix only what is broken. Add features incrementally. Do not refactor unless explicitly asked.
  • Match the surrounding code's style, naming, and comment density. Write code that reads like it was already there.
  • Do not add lines that don't buy clarity or reliability.

docs.title: 7. Where to go next
docs.text:
• Repo map — find anything, fast.
  • Contracts and invariants — the protected surfaces in full.
  • Doing a safe change — the step-by-step playbook.
  • When to ask humans — the escalation list.
  • Existing tooling — skills, MCPs, hooks already available.
  • Failure modes — recovering from red CI / lint / review.
  • Artifacts index — where binaries and data live.

The human-facing handbook (../humans/) is also worth your time — the science page explains why the axioms exist, which makes them much harder to break by accident.

docs.title: Repo Map — Every Directory, One Line
docs.text: Audience: an AI agent (Claude / Cowork / Opus) about to make a change. Hot files are flagged with 🔥 — touch carefully. Cold files are flagged with 🧊 — safe to assume nobody read them recently. Protected files are 🛡️ — do not modify without explicit approval.
